utopia.description: The book that named a genre: a sailor describes the strange island commonwealth of Utopia, where property is common, gold is worthless, and everything about European society is quietly turned on its head.
long_description: A traveler named Raphael Hythloday has seen a commonwealth unlike any in Europe: the island of Utopia, where property is held in common, gold is used for chamber pots, lawyers are banned, and six hours of work a day suffices for everyone. The author himself appears as a character in his own book — he listens, objects, and lets Raphael talk himself into the most subversive travelogue of the Renaissance.

Published in Latin in 1516, the book gave its name to an entire genre of imagined ideal societies. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Utopia Woodcut (Holbein, 1518).jpg

subjects: Political science -- Philosophy -- Early works to 1800
the-prince.description: The infamous handbook of power politics: how princes win states, keep them, and why it is safer to be feared than loved.
long_description: Written by a fired Florentine diplomat with nothing left to lose, this short book told princes what no one had dared put in writing: that politics is not a branch of morality. Whether to keep faith when it costs you, why armed prophets win and unarmed prophets fail, what fortune controls and what skill can seize — every question is answered with brutal, cheerful clarity and examples torn from the Italian wars.

Five centuries later it remains the most notorious book of political advice ever written. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Cesare Borgia, Duke of Valentinois.jpg

meditations.description: The private notebook of a Roman emperor: reminders to himself about how to stay calm, do his duty, and face death without fear.
long_description: The most powerful man in the world kept a journal that was never meant to be published. In camp on the Danube frontier, between plagues and wars, he wrote himself the same stern, humane reminders again and again: you could die at any moment, other people's opinions are not your business, and the only thing fully in your control is your own character.

That notebook became the central text of Stoic philosophy and a bedside book for readers from generals to prisoners. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Dernières paroles de l'empereur Marc-Aurèle - Eugène Delacroix.jpg
cover.painting: Last Words of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius

candide.description: A wide-eyed young man raised to believe this is the best of all possible worlds gets kicked around the entire globe testing the theory.
long_description: Candide is thrown out of the only home he knows, conscripted, flogged, shipwrecked, nearly eaten, and reunited with people he watched die — all while his tutor Pangloss insists that everything is for the best in this best of all possible worlds. The satire moves at sprint pace across three continents, demolishing optimism, war, religion, and colonial greed in a few thousand ruthless, hilarious words.

Published under a fake name in 1759 and banned almost immediately, it became the most read work of the French Enlightenment. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Fragonard, The Swing.jpg

flatland.description: A Square living in a two-dimensional world is visited by a Sphere from the third dimension, and nothing about his flat life survives the encounter.
long_description: In Flatland everyone is a shape: women are lines, workmen are triangles, gentlemen are polygons, and the priestly circles run everything. A Square narrates this geometry of society until the night a Sphere lifts him out of his plane and shows him a direction he has no word for — then asks the question that ruins him: if two dimensions can open into three, why should three be the end?

Published in 1884, it is at once a social satire, a mathematics lesson, and the founding classic of dimensional science fiction. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Kandinsky - Composition 8, July 1923.jpg

true-history.description: The original science fiction: a voyage to the moon, war between the sun and moon empires, and life inside a whale — narrated by an author who promises that every word is a lie.
long_description: A ship sails past the Pillars of Hercules, gets thrown to the moon by a whirlwind, and lands in the middle of an interplanetary war fought by vulture-cavalry and spider-troops. The narrator opens by declaring that, unlike other historians, he will tell you honestly that nothing in his book ever happened — and then delivers the most inventive pack of lies in ancient literature.

Written in the second century AD as a parody of tall-tale travel writers, it is the earliest surviving story of space travel and alien war. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Melies color Voyage dans la lune.jpg

subjects: Short stories, Italian -- Translations into English
decameron.description: Ten young Florentines flee the Black Death and pass ten days telling a hundred stories — bawdy, tragic, scandalous, and very much alive.
long_description: Florence, 1348: the plague is killing a city, so seven women and three men retreat to a country villa and agree to a game — each tells one story a day for ten days. The hundred tales that follow swing from con artists and lecherous friars to shipwrecked lovers and patient wives, told with an appetite for human weakness that no medieval book had dared before.

The collection became the model for storytellers from Chaucer onward. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:1916-a-tale-from-the-decameron-.jpg

dialogues.description: Socrates in conversation: the trial, the prison cell, the drinking party, and the relentless questions that got him executed.
long_description: A barefoot old man wanders Athens asking people to define the words they use every day — piety, justice, courage, love — and proves, politely and devastatingly, that nobody can. These dialogues follow him through the market, a dinner party, his trial for corrupting the youth, and the cell where he drinks the hemlock, arguing to the last breath that the unexamined life is not worth living.

Written by his most famous student, they are the founding documents of Western philosophy. This Modern Classics edition retells the texts in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:David - The Death of Socrates.jpg

common-sense.description: The pamphlet that talked a continent into independence: a plain-language demolition of monarchy and the case for an American republic.
long_description: In January 1776 most American colonists still hoped for reconciliation with Britain. Then a recently arrived English corset-maker published a shilling pamphlet arguing that kings are absurd, that an island should not rule a continent, and that the time to separate was now. Within months it had sold through the colonies at a rate no book in American history has matched since.

It remains the most effective piece of political persuasion ever printed in English. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Declaration of Independence (1819), by John Trumbull.jpg

federalist.description: The eighty-five newspaper essays that sold a skeptical public on the United States Constitution — and still explain how it is supposed to work.
long_description: When the Constitution left Philadelphia in 1787, ratification was anything but certain. Under the shared pen name Publius, three founders wrote a barrage of newspaper essays for New York readers: why union beats confederation, why faction can be tamed but never cured, why the presidency is not a king, and why judges must guard the Constitution against the legislature itself.

The essays remain the single most cited commentary on the American constitutional design. This Modern Classics edition retells all eighty-five papers in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Ferdinand Richardt - Independence Hall in Philadelphia - Google Art Project.jpg

democracy.description: A young French aristocrat tours Jacksonian America and writes the sharpest account of democracy — its energy, its conformity, and its future — ever produced by a visitor.
long_description: Officially he came to study American prisons. What the twenty-five-year-old French magistrate actually studied was everything else: town meetings, juries, newspapers, religion, the tyranny of majority opinion, and the restless equality that he believed was coming for Europe next, whether Europe wanted it or not.

This first volume of 1835 examines America's political machinery — and predicts, among much else, the rise of America and Russia as rival world powers. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:George Caleb Bingham - The County Election - 44-2001 - Saint Louis Art Museum.jpg

democracy2.description: The second volume of the great French study of America: how equality reshapes not just politics but thought, feeling, art, ambition — and how democracies can drift into a soft new despotism.
long_description: Five years after anatomizing America's political institutions, the author returned with a deeper question: what does equality do to the inside of people? This second volume of 1840 traces democracy's fingerprints on philosophy, religion, poetry, wages, families, and manners — and closes with the most famous warning in political literature, about a mild, providential despotism that reduces citizens to a flock of timid and industrious animals, of which the government is the shepherd.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:George Caleb Bingham - Stump Speaking.jpg

social-contract.description: "Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains" — the book that asked what could make political authority legitimate, and helped ignite a revolution.
long_description: If no man has natural authority over another, why should anyone obey a government? The answer proposed here — that legitimate power can rest only on an agreement among the people themselves, expressing a general will that no king can represent — was explosive enough that the book was banned and burned within weeks of publication in 1762.

A generation later its language was in the mouths of French revolutionaries, and it has stayed in the bloodstream of democratic argument ever since. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Jacques Louis David - Le serment du Jeu de Paume - Google Art Project.jpg

leviathan.description: Written in the shadow of civil war: why life without government is solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short — and why we trade liberty for a sovereign to escape it.
long_description: England had just executed its king when this book appeared, arguing from first principles — matter, motion, appetite, fear — that human beings in their natural state are at war with everyone, and that the only exit is a covenant erecting an artificial man, a mortal god, the great Leviathan of the commonwealth, whose sword makes promises mean something.

It is the founding masterpiece of modern political philosophy, equal parts geometry, psychology, and menace. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Leviathan by Thomas Hobbes.jpg

two-treatises.description: The philosophical foundation of liberal government: natural rights, property earned by labor, rule by consent, and the people's right to dissolve a government that betrays its trust.
long_description: The first treatise demolishes the fashionable theory that kings inherit their authority from Adam. The second builds something to replace it: men are born free and equal, property arises when labor is mixed with nature, government exists only by consent to protect life, liberty, and estate — and when it turns tyrant, rebellion is not a crime but a right.

Published anonymously in 1689, its sentences resurfaced almost verbatim in the American Declaration of Independence. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:John Constable - Wivenhoe Park, Essex - Google Art Project.jpg

wealth-of-nations.description: The book that invented economics: the division of labor, the invisible hand, and a sweeping case that nations grow rich through freedom of trade, not hoards of gold.
long_description: It opens in a pin factory, where ten workers dividing eighteen operations make thousands of pins a day that alone they could barely make at all — and from that observation builds the whole machinery of markets: prices, wages, stock, banks, rent, and the famous invisible hand by which men pursuing their own gain promote an end which was no part of their intention.

Published in 1776, it dismantled the mercantile system and founded political economy as a science. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Claude Lorrain - Seaport with the Embarkation of the Queen of Sheba - WGA05002.jpg
cover.painting: Seaport with the Embarkation of the Queen of Sheba

malthus.description: The most alarming arithmetic ever published: population grows geometrically, food arithmetically, and the gap is settled by misery — an argument that has haunted optimists since 1798.
long_description: Against the perfectibilian dreams of Godwin and Condorcet, a quiet English curate set two ratios: people multiply like compound interest, but harvests grow by addition. From that simple mismatch he derived checks that operate everywhere and always — famine, disease, war, and vice — and concluded that no utopia can outrun the pressure of population against food.

The essay reshaped economics, inspired the theory of natural selection, and made its author's name an adjective. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Pieter Bruegel the Elder- The Harvesters - Google Art Project.jpg

progress-and-poverty.description: Why does poverty deepen as wealth explodes? A self-taught San Francisco printer's answer — land rent — became the best-selling economics book of the nineteenth century.
long_description: The puzzle is stated on the first page: everywhere the machinery of production improves, and everywhere want keeps pace with progress. The answer found here is that the gains of every advance are swallowed by the rising rent of land — and the remedy, a single tax on land values, lit a worldwide political movement that counted Tolstoy, Churchill, and Einstein among its admirers.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:YCBA London a Pilgrimage 06.jpg

origin-of-species.description: One long argument that changed biology forever: variation, inheritance, and the struggle for existence add up to natural selection — and to the common descent of all living things.
long_description: Twenty years of pigeon-fancying, barnacle dissection, and worldwide correspondence went into this one long argument, published in 1859 only when a letter from the Malay Archipelago threatened to scoop it. Domestic breeds, island finches, fossil strata, and the geometry of exponential increase converge on a single idea — descent with modification through natural selection — defended against every objection its author could imagine being raised.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Martin Johnson Heade, Cattleya Orchid and Three Hummingbirds, 1871, NGA 61244.jpg

subjects: Knowledge, Theory of -- Early works to 1800
descartes.description: "I think, therefore I am": the philosophical works that doubted everything — the senses, the world, even mathematics — to find the one certainty that could rebuild knowledge.
long_description: A soldier-mathematician shut himself in a stove-heated room and resolved to demolish every opinion he had ever accepted, to see what would survive. What survived was the thinking self — and from that famous foothold he rebuilt God, the world, and a method for reasoning that promised anyone with patience could reach the truth.

These works launched modern philosophy and the analytic geometry that still bears his name. This Modern Classics edition retells the texts in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Frans Hals, Portrait of René Descartes.jpg

gallic-war.description: A general's own account of conquering Gaul: eight years of campaigns, written in the third person by the man who fought them — part war report, part campaign ad.
long_description: Gaul is divided into three parts, and over eight ruthless years its conqueror took them all — bridging the Rhine in ten days to terrify the Germans, crossing to Britain twice for the spectacle of it, and finally starving the great rebellion of Vercingetorix out of the hilltop fortress of Alesia. He wrote it all up himself, in cool third-person dispatches sent home to a Rome he was preparing to master next.

It is the only great war memoir written by an author of the ancient world about his own campaigns. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Lionel Royer. Vercingétorix devant César. G.41040.jpg
cover.painting: Vercingetorix Throws Down His Arms at the Feet of Julius Caesar

herodotus.description: The first history book ever written: the Persian Wars, plus every marvel, custom, and gossip-worthy story its insatiably curious author picked up along the way.
long_description: The project was unprecedented: find out why Greeks and Persians fought, and write it down so that great deeds would not fade with time. The result wanders gloriously — Egyptian mummification, Scythian burial mounds, gold-digging ants, the wives of Babylon — before narrowing to the story's tightest pass: a king who whipped the sea, three hundred Spartans, and the battles that saved Greece.

He has been called the Father of History and the Father of Lies; both titles are earned. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Leónidas en las Termópilas, por Jacques-Louis David.jpg

peloponnesian-war.description: The war that broke Greece, told by a general who fought in it: plague, massacre, the Melian dialogue, and the doomed expedition to Sicily — history written as a possession for all time.
long_description: An Athenian general, exiled for arriving one day too late to save a city, spent the rest of his life writing the war between Athens and Sparta with a coldness no historian has matched since: the plague that killed Pericles, the revolutions that redefined words themselves, the envoys who told a small island that the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must.

He promised a possession for all time rather than a prize essay for the moment, and delivered. This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Plague in an Ancient City LACMA AC1997.10.1 (2 of 2).jpg

montaigne.description: The invention of the essay: a retired French magistrate turns his pen on himself — on friendship, fear, cannibals, thumbs, and death — and finds the whole human condition there.
long_description: At thirty-eight he retired to his tower library, had his favorite maxims painted on the beams, and began to write about the only subject he claimed to know: himself. What do I know? became his motto, and the trials — essais — he made of his own judgment ranged over everything from the education of children to the custom of wearing clothes, always circling back to the art of living and dying well.

He invented a literary form and remains its most companionable practitioner. This Modern Classics edition retells selected essays in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Rembrandt Harmensz. van Rijn 038.jpg

subjects: Conduct of life -- Early works to 1800
enchiridion.description: The Stoic pocket manual: fifty-one short drills for keeping your head — sort what is up to you from what isn't, and want what actually happens.
long_description: Its author was born enslaved in the Roman Empire, walked with a lame leg, and taught that no one could ever really harm him — because the only things that matter, our judgments and choices, are the only things fully in our power. His student Arrian condensed the lectures into this little handbook of drills: the broken cup, the voyage and the captain’s call, the actor handed a role, the two handles. It was a bedside book for Marcus Aurelius and for the American founders alike.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Epicteti Enchiridion Latinis versibus adumbratum (Oxford 1715) frontispiece.jpg
cover.painting: the frontispiece of Edward Ivie’s Latin Enchiridion

subjects: Conduct of life -- Early works to 1800
Saving and investment -- Early works to 1800
way-to-wealth.description: Twenty-five years of Poor Richard's proverbs stitched into one speech about work, thrift, and debt — delivered to a crowd that applauds every word and then ignores it.
long_description: For a quarter century, Poor Richard's Almanack scattered its maxims across the American colonies — early to bed and early to rise, no gains without pains, the sleeping fox catches no poultry. In 1758 its author gathered the best of them into a single sly performance: an old man lectures a crowd waiting for an auction on why they can't afford the very things they're about to buy. It became the most reprinted piece of American writing of its century, the founding text of the self-improvement shelf, and — thanks to its wry frame — a joke at the sermon's own expense.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Joseph Siffred Duplessis - Benjamin Franklin - Google Art Project.jpg

memorabilia.description: Socrates remembered by his other student: not the ironist of Plato's dialogues but the practical teacher — on friendship, work, self-control, and how to be useful to your city.
long_description: Plato was not the only student taking notes. The soldier-historian who also studied with Socrates remembered a different man: less irony, more usefulness — a teacher who told a shy man to enter politics and an overconfident twenty-year-old to stay out, reconciled quarreling brothers, advised a courtesan on friendship, and answered the death-penalty charges against him with his whole way of life. It contains the Choice of Heracles — the fable of Virtue and Vice at the crossroads that John Adams wanted on the Great Seal of the United States.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Annibale Carracci - The Choice of Heracles - WGA4416.jpg

de-officiis.description: Cicero's last book, written as a letter to his son: what the right requires, what advantage tempts, and why nothing dishonorable is ever truly useful.
long_description: Months after Caesar's assassination — and months before his own — Rome's greatest orator wrote his son a book-length letter on how to act. Book by book it builds a working ethics: what the four virtues demand, how to win real advantage without shame, and, in the famous finale, why every apparent conflict between the right and the useful is an illusion, argued through cases that still bite — the grain merchant at Rhodes, the ring of invisibility, Regulus keeping his promise to his executioners. It became the founders' standard manual of public ethics, and this edition is translated directly from the original Latin.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Cicero Denounces Catiline in the Roman Senate by Cesare Maccari.png

autobiography.description: The original American life story: a runaway apprentice becomes printer, scientist, and statesman — told with the dry wit of a man who brags and then winks at the brag.
long_description: A chandler's tenth son slips out of Boston at seventeen with three puffy rolls under his arms, and proceeds to invent the American success story: print shop, newspaper, almanac, library, fire company, stove, lightning rod, and a famous scheme for achieving moral perfection in thirteen weekly steps (he settles, cheerfully, for a speckled ax). Written as a letter to his son and left unfinished at the eve of the Revolution, it is the founding document of the self-made life — and funnier than its reputation suggests.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Benjamin West, English (born America) - Benjamin Franklin Drawing Electricity from the Sky - Google Art Project.jpg
cover.painting: Benjamin Franklin Drawing Electricity from the Sky

subjects: Conduct of life -- Early works to 1800
tusculan.description: Five days of dialogues at a country villa on the things that scare us most — death, pain, grief, and the passions — ending with the case that virtue alone is enough for happiness.
long_description: In the worst year of his life — his daughter dead, his republic in one man's hands — Rome's greatest orator retreated to his villa at Tusculum and spent five days arguing himself back to his feet. One day per fear: that death is no evil, that pain can be endured, that grief is not obligatory, that the soul's disorders have cures, and that virtue by itself is enough for a happy life. It contains the Sword of Damocles, and the author's own proud account of finding Archimedes' forgotten tomb. Translated directly from the original Latin.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:The Sword of Damocles by Richard Westall.jpg

ethics.description: The original inquiry into the good life: happiness as the goal, virtue as trained habit aiming at the mean, two books on friendship, and the case for the thinking life.
long_description: What is the good life? These lecture notes — compiled for the philosopher's son — answer with the most influential ethics ever written: happiness is an activity of the soul in accordance with virtue; virtue is a habit, built by practice, aiming at the mean between too much and too little; no one becomes good by accident. Along the way: why one swallow does not make a spring, why anyone can get angry but getting angry well is rare, what friends are for, and why the life of the mind is the happiest one available to us.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:"The School of Athens" by Raffaello Sanzio da Urbino.jpg

roman-lives.description: The fall of the Roman Republic in five lives — Caesar, Cato, Cicero, Brutus, and Antony — by the ancient world's greatest biographer.
long_description: The ancient world's greatest biographer believed a jest or a small incident reveals more character than a battle, and these five lives prove it: the young hostage joking with pirates about crucifying them (he wasn't joking), the stubbornest man in Rome choosing death over Caesar's pardon, the orator who could not stop making enemies with his wit, the honorable assassin, and the general who threw away the world for Cleopatra. Together they tell the fall of the Roman Republic — the story the American founders read as a warning — five times over, from five angles.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Jean-Léon Gérôme - The Death of Caesar - Walters 37884.jpg

subjects: Conduct of life -- Early works to 1800
seneca.description: All 124 letters from a Stoic: an old philosopher writes his friend a daily course in living — time, friendship, fear, fortune, and how to die well.
long_description: Nero's ex-minister spent his forced retirement writing letters to a friend in Sicily, and accidentally invented a genre: philosophy as daily correspondence. Each letter starts somewhere real — a noisy apartment over a bathhouse, a bout of illness, a visit to a crumbling villa — and lands somewhere permanent: guard your time, choose your company, practice poverty before it practices on you, and treat the enslaved as what they are, men. Complete in all 124 letters, ending, as its author did, unafraid.
This Modern Classics edition retells the complete text in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:Manuel Domínguez Sánchez - El suicidio de Séneca.jpg

catos-letters.description: Eighteen selected letters on liberty, power, and free speech — the anonymous newspaper columns that taught the American colonies how to argue for revolution.
long_description: After the South Sea Bubble ruined half of England, two anonymous writers signing themselves "Cato" began publishing weekly letters so incendiary that the government tried to prosecute the printer. Their doctrine — free speech is the bulwark of every other liberty, power always encroaches and must always be watched, government exists only by consent and only for the governed — crossed the Atlantic and became the common sense of the American Revolution; a sixteen-year-old Benjamin Franklin was reprinting them in Boston within a year. This selection gathers the eighteen letters the founders' generation quoted most.
This Modern Classics edition retells the selected letters in contemporary conversational English, faithful to the original meaning but meant to be genuinely fun to read.

cover.commons: File:The South Sea Scheme (BM 1841,0809.230).jpg
